questions.question: Explain the difference between Quality Assurance and Quality Control. Give examples of activities in each.
keyPoints: QA is process-oriented, prevents defects; QC is product-oriented, detects defects
QA activities: process audits, training, documentation review
QC activities: testing, inspection, defect reporting
Example: QA ensures testing process is followed; QC actually performs the tests

questions.question: List and briefly explain the six quality characteristics defined in ISO 9126 standard.
keyPoints: Functionality: Suitability, Accuracy, Interoperability
Reliability: Maturity, Fault tolerance, Recoverability
Usability: Understandability, Learnability, Operability
Efficiency: Time behavior, Resource utilization
Maintainability: Analyzability, Changeability, Stability, Testability
Portability: Adaptability, Installability, Conformance, Replaceability

questions.question: What is the Capability Maturity Model (CMM)? Explain its five maturity levels.
keyPoints: CMM is a framework for measuring organizational maturity in software processes
Level 1 - Initial: Ad-hoc, chaotic processes
Level 2 - Repeatable: Basic project management processes exist
Level 3 - Defined: Processes are standardized and documented
Level 4 - Quantitatively Managed: Quantitative performance goals established
Level 5 - Optimizing: Continuous process improvement based on quantitative feedback

questions.question: Explain the Fishbone (Ishikawa) diagram. How is it used for quality improvement?
keyPoints: Also known as cause-and-effect or fishbone diagram
Used for root cause analysis of quality problems
Structure: Horizontal line with 'head' showing the problem/effect
Six categories of causes: Man, Machine, Method, Material, Measurement, Environment
Teams brainstorm causes under each category to identify root causes

questions.question: What is Six Sigma? Explain the DMAIC methodology.
keyPoints: Six Sigma is a data-driven methodology for eliminating defects
Target: 3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)
DMAIC stands for: Define (problem), Measure (current performance), Analyze (root cause), Improve (implement solution), Control (sustain improvements)
Uses statistical tools and techniques

questions.question: Define Defect Density. How is it calculated? What is a typical acceptable value?
keyPoints: Defect Density = Total Number of Defects / Size of Software (in KLOC)
Formula: DD = Total Defects / KLOC
Typical acceptable values: 1-10 defects/KLOC (depends on project criticality)
Lower defect density indicates higher quality
Used for comparing software quality across projects

questions.question: Explain the PDCA cycle and its significance in software quality management.
keyPoints: PDCA stands for Plan-Do-Check-Act
Plan: Identify improvement opportunities and plan actions
Do: Implement the planned improvements on a small scale
Check: Analyze results and compare with expected outcomes
Act: Standardize successful changes or restart the cycle
Significance: Provides continuous improvement model for quality enhancement

questions.question: Explain McCall's Quality Model in detail. Discuss the three categories of quality factors and their criteria.
keyPoints: McCall's model was developed in 1977 at IBM
11 Quality Factors categorized into three groups:
Product Operation (how software operates): Correctness, Reliability, Efficiency, Integrity, Usability
Product Revision (how software is maintained): Maintainability, Testability, Flexibility
Product Transition (how software adapts to new environments): Portability, Reusability, Interoperability
Each factor has associated quality criteria
Model helps in setting quality goals and measuring achievements

questions.question: Compare ISO 9126 and ISO 25010 standards for software quality. What are the enhancements in ISO 25010?
keyPoints: ISO 9126 has 6 characteristics: Functionality, Reliability, Usability, Efficiency, Maintainability, Portability
ISO 25010 replaces ISO 9126 with 8 characteristics
ISO 25010 introduces: Security (new), and restructures some characteristics
Functionality becomes Functional Suitability
Usability splits into Usability and Accessibility
Reliability becomes Reliability and Maintainability becomes Maintainability with additional sub-characteristics
ISO 25010 includes internal/external metrics and quality in use model
ISO 25010 is more comprehensive for modern software systems

questions.question: Discuss Total Quality Management (TQM) principles and explain how they can be applied in software development organizations.
keyPoints: TQM is a management philosophy for continuous quality improvement

Eight fundamental principles: Customer-focused organization, Total employee involvement, Process approach, Integrated system, Strategic and systematic approach, Continuous improvement, Fact-based decision making, Communication

Application in software: Customer requirements gathering, Team training and empowerment, Define software processes, Quality management system implementation, Regular process audits, Use of metrics for decision making, Effective communication channels
Benefits: Improved product quality, higher customer satisfaction, reduced rework, better employee morale

questions.question: Explain the concept of software quality metrics. Discuss at least five important metrics used in the industry with their formulas and significance.
keyPoints: Quality metrics are quantitative measures of software quality attributes
Five important metrics:
1. Defect Density = Defects / KLOC - measures code quality
2. Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) = Total operational time / Number of failures - measures reliability
3. Test Coverage = (Tested requirements / Total requirements) × 100% - measures test completeness
4. Code Review Efficiency = Defects found in review / Total defects - measures review process
5. Customer Satisfaction Index = Survey responses analysis - measures user satisfaction
6. Productivity = KLOC / Person-month - measures development efficiency
These metrics help in objective quality assessment and improvement
